title: Protecting Your Application
text: Protecting Your Application Sensitive data needs to be protected against attacks and theft from unauthorized parties. Uniface provides a variety of ways in which to do this. Uniface provides the following tools and techniques for protecting your data in both client/server and web applications: Support for TLS and HTTPS protocols, which use digital certificates to protect data over network and internet communication channels. For more information, see TLS/SSL Protocol and HTTPS Protocol and Digital Certificates . The TLS network connector for encrypted data transport between the Uniface Router, its Uniface Servers, and client Uniface applications, using the Transport Layer Security (TLS) protocol. For more information, see TLS Connector . Pathscrambler utility to encrypt sensitive information such as user names, passwords, machine names, and so. It can be used to encrypt information in assignment files and in strings used in Java servlet settings, the open Proc statement, and the /log (login) command line switch. For more information, see Pathscrambler . Triggers formatToDbms and formatFromDbms can be used to encrypt and decrypt data when it is stored and retrieved from a database. For more information, see trigger formatToDbms and trigger formatFromDbms . Proc functions $encode and $decode can be used to create your own encryption and decryption routines using a variety of commonly used algorithms, including cryptographic hash functions, hash message authentication codes (HMAC), RSA, DSA, and block ciphers. For more information, see $encode and $decode and Supported Cryptography Algorithms . 3GL encryption functions can be used in C and C++ components that integrate with Uniface. For more information, see 3GL Encryption Functions . Customizable security drivers. Security drivers for client/server can encode and decode connect messages and message header data that are exchanged between the Uniface client and Uniface Router. Security drivers for SMTP Authentication can negotiate SMTP authentication via a Simple Authentication and Security Layer (SASL) mechanism. For more information, see Security Drivers and the Security Driver API . Other built-in security features. For example, Uniface protects non-editable fields in web applications using a salted MD5 hash. Web applications face additional security threats. For more information, see Web Security Guidelines . Related Links $encode Encrypt data, sign messages with digital signatures, or convert data from one encoding scheme to another. $decode Decrypt or decode data, or verify a message by means of a digital signature.
